1: David and his men arrive at Ziklag on the third day to find it burned — the Amalekites have raided while the men were away with the Philistine muster. Ziklag (possibly Tell esh-Shariya/Tel Sera) was on the southern frontier of Judahite territory, in the Negev — exposed to raids from the south and east. The Amalekites operated in the same southern desert regions David had been raiding (1 Samuel 27:8-12). The raid is a strategic counter-response: while David raided Amalekite territory, they struck his home base in his absence.
2: The Amalekites did not kill anyone but took everyone captive — women and children from young to old. The selective taking of captives without killing reflects the economic logic of slave raiding in the ancient Near East: living captives were worth substantially more than corpses, both for ransom and for sale into slavery. The Amalekite raiders were conducting a profitable abduction operation, not a vendetta.
3: David and his men come to the city — it is burned and their wives and sons and daughters have been taken captive. The full scope of the disaster: every family in the entire Ziklag garrison has been taken. The 600 men David commanded (1 Samuel 23:13) are all simultaneously bereaved of their families. The communal catastrophe creates the conditions for the communal grief and near-mutiny of v4-6.
4: David and the people weep until there is no more strength in them to weep. The complete exhaustion of grief — weeping until physically unable to continue — is a psychologically precise description of acute communal trauma. The men have marched for three days from Aphek, arrived to destroyed homes, and now mourn while too exhausted to act.
5: David's two wives — Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail the widow of Nabal of Carmel — have also been taken. The personal dimension of the raid intersects with the communal: David has lost not just his men's families but his own household. His leadership role requires him to lead while personally mourning the same loss as his men.
6: David is in great distress because the people speak of stoning him — each man is bitter in soul for his sons and daughters. The near-mutiny is the logical outcome of 600 men simultaneously blaming their leader for the disaster. David's decision to bring them on the Philistine muster created the tactical opportunity for the raid. David's response: he strengthens himself in YHWH his God (wayyitḥazzēq dāwîḏ baYHWH ʾĕlōhāyw). This is the first explicit reference to David's personal spiritual resource in extremity — the source of his resilience is theological, not merely psychological.
7: David inquires of YHWH through Abiathar and the ephod: 'Shall I pursue this raiding party? Shall I overtake them?' YHWH answers: 'Pursue, for you shall surely overtake and shall surely rescue.' The ephod consultation in crisis is David's consistent pattern (1 Samuel 23:2,4,9-12). The oracle is unambiguous and confident — the unusual double infinitive absolute (nāḵôn tassîg wəhassēl tassîl) emphasizes certainty. David acts immediately on the oracle.
8: David sets out with the 600 men and comes to the Wadi Besor. The Besor is a seasonal stream in the northern Negev (modern Nahal Besor) approximately 25 km south of Ziklag. Its significance is as a major obstacle in the southern Negev — its steep banks and the exhaustion of the men make it a natural stopping point for those who cannot continue.
9: Two hundred men are too exhausted to cross the Besor and stay behind; the remaining 400 continue the pursuit. The exhaustion of a third of the force after the three-day march from Aphek plus the emotional trauma of Ziklag's destruction is physiologically realistic. The 400 who continue are those with reserves sufficient to sustain the pursuit; the 200 who stay are not cowards but men at their physical limit.
10: David goes on with 400 men. The division of forces and the subsequent controversy about spoil-sharing (v21-25) establishes the specific military administrative question that David's ruling will address. The narrative preserves the exact numbers because they matter for the legal precedent.
11: They find an Egyptian in the field and bring him to David. He is given bread to eat and water to drink — the provision of food and water before questioning follows the ANE practice of establishing hospitality obligations before interrogating. A man who has received food and water is under the household's protection and is expected to respond faithfully to questions.
12: The Egyptian is fed — figs, raisins, water — and his spirit revives after three days without food. He is an Amalekite slave who had been abandoned when he became sick during the raid. The abandoned slave is YHWH's providential guide: the one discarded by the enemy becomes the one who leads David to them. The pattern of the enemy's cast-off becoming the instrument of their defeat recurs throughout biblical narrative.
13: The Egyptian is a servant of an Amalekite. His master left him behind when he fell ill three days ago. The three-day gap between the Amalekite departure and David's arrival means the raiding party is at most three days' travel ahead — still catchable if the 400 can close the distance quickly. The Egyptian knows the route and the destination.
14: The Egyptian confirms the scope of the raid: they struck the Negev of the Cherethites, of Judah, and of Caleb, and burned Ziklag with fire. The Cherethites are the Aegean-origin peoples settled in the southern coastal plain (cognate with Cretans — Philistine-related peoples). The geographic spread of the raid — Cherethite, Judahite, Calebite territory — indicates an opportunistic wide-ranging operation, not a targeted strike on Ziklag alone.
15: David asks him to lead them to the raiding party; the Egyptian requests a sworn guarantee of life and non-extradition to his master. The swearing of protection (hiššābaʿ lî bēʾlōhîm) before information-giving is a formal legal arrangement — the guide is requesting asylum before acting as informant. David swears, the Egyptian leads.
16: They arrive to find the Amalekites scattered across the land — eating, drinking, and celebrating over the great plunder from Philistia and Judah. The victory feast of the raiders, spread across the landscape, makes them vulnerable: no defensive formation, no scouts posted, no anticipation of pursuit at this distance. The Amalekites' confidence in their escape has become their tactical undoing.
17: David strikes them from dusk until the evening of the next day — a continuous battle lasting approximately 24 hours. Only 400 men escape on camels. The use of camels for flight reflects the desert-raiding culture of the Amalekites and related groups — camels provided rapid long-distance escape through terrain impassable for infantry. The escape of 400 on camels means the raiding force was substantially larger than 400 total.
18: David recovers everything the Amalekites had taken, including his two wives. The full recovery — nothing lost — fulfills the oracle's promise ('you shall surely rescue'). The narrative notes the completeness of the recovery before dealing with the spoil-controversy, establishing that David's leadership has been vindicated before the internal conflict is addressed.
19: Nothing is missing — small or great, sons or daughters, spoil or anything that had been taken — David brings it all back. The totality of the recovery is emphasized by the fourfold enumeration (small/great, sons/daughters, spoil/whatever taken). The oracle's double certainty ('surely overtake, surely rescue') has been fulfilled in every particular.
20: David also takes all the flocks and herds, which were driven before the other livestock. The spoil of the Amalekite raid — beyond the recovered captives — includes substantial animal wealth from their original plundering of multiple territories. 'This is David's spoil' — the attribution of the entire recovered wealth to David personally sets up the administrative question of distribution.
21: David returns to the 200 men who were too exhausted to cross the Besor. They come to meet David and the people; David greets them warmly. The greeting — wayyišʾal lāhem lĕšālôm, he inquired of their wellbeing — is the formal greeting that treats the 200 as valued members of the group, not as failures who stayed behind.
22: The wicked and worthless men (ʾanšê hārāʿ wəhabbəlîyyaʿal) among the 400 say the 200 who stayed behind should receive only their wives and children and not share in the spoil. The objection has military-legal precedent: in many ancient armies, spoil was distributed only to those who fought. The counter-position David will articulate (v23-24) is a new ruling that establishes a different standard for Israel.
23: David replies: 'You shall not do so, my brothers, with what YHWH has given us. He has preserved us and given into our hand the raiding party that came against us.' David's argument is theological before it is legal: the victory belongs to YHWH, not to the 400 fighters. If YHWH gave the victory, the distribution belongs to YHWH's community — including those who guarded the baggage.
24: 'Who would listen to you in this matter? For as his share is who goes down into the battle, so shall his share be who stays by the baggage. They shall share alike.' The ruling — equal shares for fighters and logistics personnel — became a standing legal principle in Israel. The same principle appears in the Talmud's rules about spoil distribution. David is not making a charitable exception but establishing a legal precedent that recognizes all contributions to a military operation.
25: David makes this a statute and ordinance for Israel from that day forward to this day. The legal formula — ḥōq ûmišpāṭ (statute and ordinance) — is the same language used for Torah regulations. David's ruling at Ziklag is his first legislative act as proto-king; he governs before he is formally installed. The Ziklag spoil-ruling is one of the foundations of Israelite military law.
26: David sends portions of the spoil to the elders of Judah, his friends, with the message: 'Here is a gift for you from the spoil of the enemies of YHWH.' The distribution to Judahite towns is political capital-building before the formal throne bid: David is cultivating relationships with the tribal leadership throughout Judah by sharing recovered spoil from common enemies. The list of towns in vv27-31 covers the Negev and southern Judah — precisely the region he will be declared king of first (2 Samuel 2:4).
27: The first towns listed — Bethel, Ramoth-Negev, Jattir — are in the southern hill country and Negev of Judah. These towns represent communities that had suffered from the same Amalekite raiding that struck Ziklag (v14 identified the Negev of Judah as part of the raid). David is restoring what was taken from these communities while simultaneously demonstrating largesse.
28: Aroer, Siphmoth, Eshtemoa — additional towns in the southern Judah/Negev region, including Eshtemoa which later appears as a Levitical city (Joshua 21:14). The distribution covers a wide geographic footprint, suggesting David's network of relationships in Judah extended throughout the region even during his time at Ziklag.
29: The distribution reaches Racal, the cities of the Jerahmeelites, and the cities of the Kenites. The Jerahmeelites and Kenites were non-Israelite groups living within Judahite territory — their inclusion in the spoil distribution reflects David's inclusive approach to building political relationships across ethnic boundaries, consistent with his later inclusive policies as king.
30: Hormah, Bor-ashan, Athach — towns in the far south. Hormah has historical resonance: it was the site of early Israelite military defeats (Numbers 14:45; 1 Samuel 21:3) and later victories. Including it in the distribution honors the full extent of Judahite identity.
31: Hebron and all the places where David and his men had roamed. Hebron receives the last mention — it will become David's first capital (2 Samuel 2:1-4). The summary 'all the places where David and his men had roamed' acknowledges that David's fugitive years had built relationships across the southern highlands that will now support his kingship. The wandering that looked like exile was actually network-building for the kingdom to come.
